1: Sarah lived to be a hundred and twenty-seven years old — the only woman in the OT whose age at death is recorded. Sarah’s age is given as a mark of honor and covenant significance: she is not merely an appendage to Abraham but a covenant figure in her own right (Genesis 17:15–16: “I will bless her… she will be the mother of nations”). Paul identifies Sarah as the covenant-matriarch whose free-woman status defines the gospel community (Galatians 4:26: “the Jerusalem that is above is free, and she is our mother”; Genesis 4:31: “we are not children of the slave woman, but of the free woman”). 1 Peter 3:6 holds Sarah up as the model of holy wifely faith. Her death at 127 closes the chapter of the promise-generation.
2: Sarah died at Kiriath Arba (Hebron) in Canaan, and Abraham went to mourn for Sarah and to weep over her — the first death of a major covenant figure in the narrative. Abraham’s mourning is grief within faith: he mourns Sarah’s death while holding the promise that was given through her. The NT pattern of grief within hope: “Brothers and sisters, we do not want you to be uninformed about those who sleep in death, so that you do not grieve like the rest of mankind, who have no hope” (1 Thessalonians 4:13). The grief is real; the hope transforms it. Christ himself weeps at Lazarus’ tomb (John 11:35) before raising him — the tears of genuine grief from the one who is “the resurrection and the life” (John 11:25).
3: Abraham rose from beside his dead wife and spoke to the Hittites — the rising from mourning to act on behalf of the dead is the covenant-faithful response. Abraham’s rising to secure burial for Sarah is an act of faith in the future: he is buying land in a country that God has promised to give him. The purchase of Machpelah is a down payment on the promised inheritance, an act of faith that the promise is real even though it has not been fully realized. Faith acts in the present on the basis of the future promise; this is the pattern of the Christian life (Hebrews 11:1: “faith is confidence in what we hope for and assurance about what we do not see”).
4: “I am a foreigner and stranger among you. Sell me some property for a burial site here so I can bury my dead” — “foreigner and stranger” (ger and toshav) is the self-description that Hebrews 11:13 makes paradigmatic for all people of faith: “they admitted that they were foreigners and strangers on earth.” 1 Peter 2:11: “Dear friends, I urge you, as foreigners and exiles, to abstain from sinful desires.” The pilgrim identity that Abraham voices before the Hittites is the identity of all who live “by faith, not by sight” (2 Corinthians 5:7), looking for the city whose architect and builder is God (Hebrews 11:10). Christ himself had no permanent earthly home (Luke 9:58) and calls his followers into the same pilgrim posture.
5: The Hittites replied to Abraham — the Gentile community’s respectful response to the covenant-bearer is a type of the openness to Abraham’s greater descendant. The Hittites treat Abraham as “a mighty prince among us” (v6) despite his self-description as a foreigner. The recognition of the covenant-bearer’s dignity by the surrounding peoples anticipates the eschatological recognition of Christ’s lordship: “at the name of Jesus every knee should bow… and every tongue acknowledge that Jesus Christ is Lord” (Philippians 2:10–11). The willing deference of the Hittites is the small-scale type of the universal acknowledgment that Christ’s lordship will one day receive.
6: “Sir, listen to us. You are a mighty prince among us. Bury your dead in the choicest of our tombs” — Abraham is recognized as a “mighty prince” (lit. “prince of God” — nesi’ elohim) by the Hittites. The recognition of Abraham’s royal-divine status by the Gentile community is a type of the recognition of Christ as the “King of kings and Lord of lords” (Revelation 19:16) that will come from all peoples. Christ is not merely a prince of God but God the Son (John 1:1; Philippians 2:6); the Hittites’ recognition of Abraham as God’s prince is the type of the universal recognition that Christ’s lordship will receive from every nation.
7: Abraham rose and bowed down before the people of the land, the Hittites — the humble posture of the covenant-bearer before the Gentile community. Abraham’s bowing in cultural respect is not compromise but courtesy — the proper negotiating posture in the ANE. Christ engages similarly with all people, accepting hospitality from those outside the covenant (Luke 7:36: dining with the Pharisee; Luke 19:5: entering Zacchaeus’s home). The covenant-bearer’s humble engagement with the surrounding culture is the model for the church’s missionary posture: “becoming all things to all people” (1 Corinthians 9:22) without compromising the covenant identity.
8: Abraham said, “If you are willing to let me bury my dead, then intercede with Ephron son of Zohar on my behalf” — Abraham seeks an intermediary to approach Ephron. The use of community elders as intermediaries in land transactions is the ANE legal norm. The use of intermediaries runs through the OT: Moses as mediator of the Sinai covenant, priests as mediators of worship, prophets as mediators of the divine word. Christ is the mediator who replaces all these: “there is one God and one mediator between God and mankind, the man Christ Jesus” (1 Timothy 2:5). The chain of intermediaries in the OT points toward and is superseded by the one Mediator of the new covenant.
9: “Ask him to sell me the cave of Machpelah, which belongs to him and is at the end of his field. Ask him to sell it to me at the full price as a burial site among you” — Abraham asks to pay full price, not to receive a gift. The insistence on paying the full price for the burial site corresponds to the gospel’s insistence that the full price of sin has been paid: “You are not your own; you were bought at a price” (1 Corinthians 6:19–20); “the precious blood of Christ, a lamb without blemish or defect” (1 Peter 1:19). Abraham’s full-price purchase is a type of the full payment of atonement: nothing partial, nothing subsidized by the seller, but the complete transaction that secures permanent ownership.
10: Ephron the Hittite replied to Abraham in the hearing of all the Hittites who had come to the gate of the city — the public negotiation at the city gate is the formal legal setting of ANE property transactions. The city gate is the site of legal proceedings throughout the OT: Boaz redeems Ruth at the gate of Bethlehem (Ruth 4:1–11); the elders judge at the gate (Deuteronomy 21:19; Genesis 22:15). The public legal nature of Abraham’s purchase gives it binding legal permanence. The new covenant is similarly made publicly: “This is what has been publicly declared: God has made Jesus both Lord and Messiah, this Jesus whom you crucified” (Acts 2:36). The covenant is a public legal transaction, not a private arrangement.
11: “No, my lord, listen to me; I give you the field, and I give you the cave that is in it. I give it to you in the presence of my people. Bury your dead” — Ephron’s initial offer of a gift rather than a sale. In ANE negotiating custom, offering to give is the first move in a negotiation that will conclude with payment. Abraham insists on paying (v12–13) because he will not be indebted to the Hittites for his covenant landholding — the same principle as his refusal of the king of Sodom’s offer (Genesis 14:23). The covenant inheritance cannot be received as a gift from those outside the covenant; it must be purchased through the covenant mediator. The new covenant inheritance is purchased by Christ, not given by the world.
12: Abraham bowed down before the people of the land — the repeated gesture of respect in the negotiation. The humble persistence of covenant-seeking in a transactional world is the pattern of prayer: “keep on asking… keep on seeking… keep on knocking” (Matthew 7:7 — the present tense imperatives). Abraham does not accept Ephron’s first offer but persists in seeking the proper transaction. The persistence in prayer that Jesus teaches (Luke 18:1–8: the persistent widow) has its type in the patriarchal persistence in covenant-negotiation. Both persist until the proper, complete transaction is secured.
13: Abraham said, “Listen to me, if you will. I will pay the price of the field. Accept it from me so I can bury my dead there” — the insistence on full payment. Abraham refuses the free gift because owning Canaan as a gift from the Hittites would undermine the covenant claim that God has promised to give it. The integrity of the covenant inheritance requires that it not be received on terms set by those outside the covenant. The new covenant inheritance — justification, adoption, eternal life — is similarly not given on terms set by human religious achievement or moral effort; it comes exclusively through Christ’s payment (Titus 3:5: “he saved us, not because of righteous things we had done, but because of his mercy”).
14: Ephron answered Abraham — the negotiation continues. The formal ANE negotiating pattern — offer, counteroffer, acceptance — is preserved in the narrative detail. The patience of the covenant-bearer in working through the proper legal channels is a type of Christ’s fulfillment of every legal requirement: “Let it be so now; it is proper for us to do this to fulfill all righteousness” (Matthew 3:15 — Christ submitting to baptism to fulfill the law’s requirements). Every proper form is honored; no legal corner is cut; the covenant is secured through the proper channels.
15: “My lord, listen to me; the land is worth four hundred shekels of silver, but what is that between you and me? Bury your dead” — Ephron names a price while appearing to minimize its significance. The four hundred shekels of silver is a substantial sum (by comparison, David pays fifty shekels for the threshing floor in 2 Samuel 24:24; Joseph is sold for twenty shekels in Genesis 37:28). The seemingly casual “what is that between you and me?” is the ANE politeness that masks the serious transaction. The full weight of the price — however minimized in language — must be paid. The full price of human sin, however minimized in human estimation, must be paid; Christ pays it in full (John 19:30: “It is finished” = the debt is paid).
16: Abraham agreed and weighed out four hundred shekels of silver according to the weight current among the merchants — the full price paid in silver, publicly, according to the standard of the marketplace. The payment at the current market rate using the merchants’ standard ensures that the transaction is binding by any external standard. The atonement Christ provides is similarly calibrated to the full standard of divine justice: “The wages of sin is death” (Romans 6:23); Christ pays the full wage. The ransom paid for many (Matthew 20:28) is not a negotiated discount but the full market rate of divine holiness satisfied by the one sacrifice of infinite worth.
17: Ephron’s field in Machpelah near Mamre — both the field and the cave, and all the trees within the borders — was deeded to Abraham as his property — the comprehensive legal description of what was purchased. The specific, legally precise description — field, cave, trees, borders — ensures that the covenant possession is exactly defined and legally unambiguous. The inheritance secured by Christ is similarly precisely defined in the NT: “an inheritance that can never perish, spoil or fade — kept in heaven for you” (1 Peter 1:4). The covenant inheritance is not vague but specific; not provisional but permanent; not conditional on human faithfulness but secured by Christ’s once-for-all purchase.
18: The field was deeded to Abraham as his property in the presence of all the Hittites who had come to the gate of the city — the public legal ratification. The full community witnesses the transfer of ownership; the covenant possession is secured by public witness, not private agreement. The public dimension of the atonement — the crucifixion done in public view, the resurrection announced publicly, the gospel proclaimed to all nations — is the new covenant’s public ratification. “God presented Christ as a sacrifice of atonement… to demonstrate his righteousness” (Romans 3:25) — the atonement is a public demonstration, not a private transaction between God and a few.
19: Abraham buried his wife Sarah in the cave in the field of Machpelah near Mamre (Hebron) in the land of Canaan — the burial of the covenant-matriarch in the promised land. Sarah is the first to be buried in Machpelah; she will be followed by Abraham (Genesis 25:9–10), Isaac and Rebekah (Genesis 49:31), Leah (Genesis 49:31), and Jacob (Genesis 50:13). The cave becomes the covenant family’s tomb. The tomb in the promised land is a statement of faith: the covenant family plants its dead in the land of promise, trusting the resurrection. Hebrews 11:13 says they “were still living by faith when they died” — dying in the promised land rather than returning to Ur. The tomb in Canaan is the type of Christ’s tomb in Jerusalem: the death planted in the promised city from which resurrection life emerges.
20: So the field and the cave were deeded to Abraham by the Hittites as a burial site — the chapter closes with the legal completion. The first permanent possession of the promised land is a burial site — a place for the dead, not the living. This is the covenant-faith inversion: the first foothold in the land of promise is claimed through death. Christ’s first permanent mark on history is also through death: the cross planted on Golgotha, the tomb sealed in the garden. But the burial site is never the final word: Isaac’s family buries their dead in Machpelah while trusting the resurrection; Christ’s tomb is emptied on the third day. The cave at Machpelah is the type of the empty tomb: death entered in faith, and the stone that seals it does not have the final word.
